mainEntity.name: 1.  Why did you want to gain a distinction with the Royal Photographic Society (RPS)?

acceptedAnswer.text: I had subscribed to RPS for many years and therefore saw successful panels and always wondered whether I could get up to that standard. At 71 I decided to put that to the test.

mainEntity.name: 2.  Why did you choose Alan to mentor you for RPS Distinctions and had you worked with Alan before the RPS Mentoring Course?

acceptedAnswer.text: I wasn’t a member of a photography club with regular competitions and consequently didn’t have much experience of selecting images against a set of criteria and for those images to be critiqued by someone else. I, therefore, needed to learn those skills. Before joining the course, I attended one of Alan’s Arboretum workshops.

mainEntity.name: 6.  What RPS Distinctions did you apply for and were you successful?

acceptedAnswer.text: I successfully submitted a panel for the Print ‘Licentiate’ Fine Art.

mainEntity.name: 7.  What was your experience like of the RPS Distinctions Assessment Day?

acceptedAnswer.text: I found the RPS staff very helpful in dealing with queries I had about the submission prior to the Assessment but I had a prior commitment on the day of the Assessment.

mainEntity.name: 9. Having completed the course and RPS Distinctions Assessment, what advice would you like to share with others?

acceptedAnswer.text: If you can accept ‘critique’ and learn from that process you should go for it! With Alan’s guidance, as a minimum, you will learn to be a better photographer and, if you acquire the necessary skills, you will have the satisfaction of your achievement being recognised.

mainEntity.name: 11.  Has working for a RPS Distinctions helped you progress as a photographer?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. That was one of the objectives and consequently, I tried to ensure that I did so across the whole range of skills required to gain the Licentiate.
